content.text: What is Cloud Platforms for Agents?

Cloud platforms for agents are the managed infrastructure layers — compute, networking, storage, queuing, and observability — that host and operate AI agents in production. Rather than running an agent process on a single server you manage yourself, you deploy agent code as containerized workloads on platforms such as
. The platform handles capacity provisioning, autoscaling, health checks, secrets injection, IAM, and regional redundancy while your code focuses exclusively on agent logic.

Agentic workloads have unusual characteristics: long-running requests (seconds to minutes), bursty parallelism during fan-out, external I/O to LLM APIs and tools, and state that must survive container restarts. Cloud platforms address each of these through managed message queues, durable object stores, autoscaling groups, and serverless containers — composable primitives you wire together rather than build from scratch.

A production agent that runs reliably on a developer's laptop often fails in subtle ways at scale: a single container cannot handle 500 concurrent research tasks, a process restart loses in-flight agent state, and hard-coded API keys in environment files become a security liability. Cloud platforms solve the operational gap between a working prototype and a production service that users and organizations can depend on.
autoscaling groups spin agent containers up and down based on queue depth or CPU, so a traffic spike does not require a human to provision servers.
a crashed container is replaced automatically; a poison-pill task is dead-lettered rather than blocking the whole queue.
managed platforms ship built-in metrics (latency, error rate, cost) with dashboards and alerting that would take weeks to build yourself.
cloud providers maintain SOC 2, HIPAA, FedRAMP, and GDPR certifications, which flow down to workloads you run inside their perimeters.
VPCs, private subnets, and load balancers are configured declaratively; containers find each other by service name without hardcoded IPs.
Cloud Run and AWS Lambda eliminate idle-server costs for low-volume agents. You pay only for the time your agent is actively executing.
for agents that embed documents locally or run inference on open-weight models, cloud providers offer on-demand GPU instances (A100, L4, H100) in the same cluster.
AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, and Azure Key Vault inject API keys at container startup, keeping them out of images and environment files.

SQS, Google Pub/Sub, and Azure Service Bus provide at-least-once delivery with configurable retention, visibility timeout, and dead-letter queues — exactly the semantics a long-running agent task requires.
cloud providers offer managed vector databases (Vertex AI Vector Search, Amazon Aurora pgvector), managed Kafka, managed Redis, and managed workflow engines — all within the same security boundary.

serverless containers (Cloud Run, Lambda) can take 1–5 seconds to start when scaled from zero. For interactive agents where the user waits on the first response, this is noticeable. Minimum instance counts or provisioned concurrency eliminate cold starts at the cost of idle billing.

every outbound HTTP call from an agent container to an LLM API or external service crosses a cloud network boundary. Egress pricing (typically $0.08–$0.12 / GB) adds up for agents that transfer large documents or call tool APIs frequently.

AWS Lambda has a 15-minute hard limit; Cloud Run tasks default to 60 minutes but support up to 24 hours only for Cloud Run Jobs. Very long-running agents (multi-hour research tasks) require either task splitting or a durable execution engine like Temporal.
using provider-specific services (DynamoDB, Firestore, Azure Cosmos DB) for agent state makes cross-cloud migration expensive. Abstracting state access behind a repository interface mitigates this.

the cloud-native model introduces a large surface area: IAM roles, VPC configuration, container registries, task definitions, and service meshes. Small teams with one or two agents may find a single VM simpler to operate.

. Each agent process is packaged as a Docker image that reads its API keys from the secrets manager at startup, polls a message queue for work, executes a single agent task, writes results to object storage or a database, and exits (or loops). No state lives inside the container. This design enables safe horizontal scaling: any number of identical containers can run concurrently because they do not share in-process state.
The lifecycle for a single agent invocation:
A client sends a request to the API Gateway (e.g., ALB or API Gateway v2). The gateway authenticates the request and routes it to an agent service endpoint.
The endpoint handler enqueues a task message on SQS / Pub/Sub (task type, input payload, correlation ID) and immediately returns
with a task ID to the caller.

A pool of agent containers (ECS tasks / Cloud Run instances) polls the queue. One container picks up the task, fetches secrets, calls the LLM API, executes tools, and writes the result to a state store keyed by task ID.
endpoint (or receives a webhook / SSE stream) to retrieve the completed result.
The autoscaler monitors queue depth. If more than N messages are visible, it scales out agent containers; when the queue drains, it scales back in.

attrs.alt: Cloud platform architecture: client sends requests through API Gateway to containerized agent pods (ECS/Cloud Run/AKS) backed by message queue, state store, object storage, secrets manager, and observability layer; agents call external LLM APIs and vector databases

content.text: Cloud platform deployment is the right choice when your agent workload has reached a scale or reliability bar that a single-server setup cannot meet. Concrete signals:
more than a handful of simultaneous agent runs that would saturate one machine's memory or CPU.
SLAs above 99% for user-facing agent features (a single VM has no automatic failover).
workloads that spike by 10x–100x (batch processing, end-of-day jobs, event-driven pipelines) benefit from autoscaling rather than over-provisioning a fixed server.
regulations (GDPR, HIPAA) that require data to stay in a specific cloud region and inherit the provider's certifications.

organizations large enough to have a platform or DevOps function that can manage cloud infrastructure. Solo projects or rapid prototypes are better served by a single VM or a PaaS like Heroku / Railway.

that installs dependencies and sets the entrypoint to your agent worker script. The container must not read secrets from environment variables at build time — it should call the secrets manager API at startup.
ECR (AWS), Artifact Registry (GCP), or ACR (Azure). Tag images with the Git commit SHA for traceability.

create an SQS FIFO queue (for ordered tasks) or standard queue (for parallel fan-out). Set a visibility timeout longer than the maximum expected task duration so a slow agent does not re-deliver its own message.
give the container task role permission to read from the queue, read secrets, and write to the state store. Grant no other permissions — least privilege applies to agent code too.
Store API keys in secrets manager:
and other credentials to AWS Secrets Manager / GCP Secret Manager. Reference the secret ARN in the task definition rather than setting the value directly.
write an ECS Task Definition (AWS), Cloud Run service YAML (GCP), or Kubernetes Deployment (Azure AKS). Set resource limits (CPU, memory) appropriate for concurrent LLM calls.
attach an Application Auto Scaling policy or KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling) rule that scales on SQS

configure structured JSON logs to CloudWatch / Cloud Logging. Emit a custom metric for each agent step (model call, tool call, retry). Create dashboards for p50/p99 latency, error rate, and cost per run.
build and push the image in a GitHub Actions or Cloud Build pipeline triggered by a merge to main. Use a rolling update to replace running containers with zero downtime.
Dockerfile for a Python agent worker

# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1
FROM python:3.12-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Install dependencies separately for layer caching
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy agent source
COPY src/ ./src/

# Non-root user for security
RUN useradd -m agent
USER agent

# The worker polls SQS and runs agent tasks.
# Secrets are fetched at startup from AWS Secrets Manager via IAM role.
CMD ["python", "-m", "src.worker"]
SQS worker loop polling for agent tasks

"""src/worker.py — SQS worker that executes one agent task per message."""

import json
import boto3
import anthropic
from src.secrets import load_secrets  # fetches from AWS Secrets Manager
from src.agent import run_agent_task

# Load secrets once at startup (IAM role grants read access)
secrets = load_secrets()
anthropics_client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=secrets["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"])

sqs = boto3.client("sqs", region_name="us-east-1")
QUEUE_URL = secrets["AGENT_QUEUE_URL"]

def poll():
    """Long-poll SQS and process one task at a time."""
    while True:
        resp = sqs.receive_message(
            QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL,
            MaxNumberOfMessages=1,        # process one task per iteration
            WaitTimeSeconds=20,           # long-polling; avoids busy loop
            VisibilityTimeout=300,        # 5 min; must exceed max task duration
        )
        messages = resp.get("Messages", [])
        if not messages:
            continue

        msg = messages[0]
        body = json.loads(msg["Body"])
        receipt_handle = msg["ReceiptHandle"]

        try:
            result = run_agent_task(anthropics_client, body["task"], body["input"])
            # Store result for the caller to retrieve
            store_result(body["task_id"], result)
            # Delete the message only on success
            sqs.delete_message(QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL, ReceiptHandle=receipt_handle)
        except Exception as exc:
            # Leave the message in queue; visibility timeout expires → retry
            print(f"Task {body['task_id']} failed: {exc}")
            # After max retries SQS moves it to the dead-letter queue

if __name__ == "__main__":
    poll()
Fetching a secret from AWS Secrets Manager at startup

"""src/secrets.py — load all agent secrets from AWS Secrets Manager."""

import json
import boto3
from botocore.exceptions import ClientError

def load_secrets(secret_name: str = "agent/production") -> dict:
    """
    Retrieve a JSON secret from AWS Secrets Manager.

    The container's IAM role must have secretsmanager:GetSecretValue on this ARN.
    Returns the secret as a dict so caller can do secrets["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"].
    """
    client = boto3.client("secretsmanager", region_name="us-east-1")
    try:
        response = client.get_secret_value(SecretId=secret_name)
        return json.loads(response["SecretString"])
    except ClientError as e:
        raise RuntimeError(f"Could not load secret '{secret_name}': {e}") from e

# cloudrun-agent.yaml
apiVersion: serving.knative.dev/v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: research-agent
  annotations:
    run.googleapis.com/ingress: internal   # no public endpoint; triggered by Pub/Sub
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        autoscaling.knative.dev/minScale: "1"   # keep one warm to avoid cold start
        autoscaling.knative.dev/maxScale: "50"  # cap to control LLM API quota usage
        run.googleapis.com/execution-environment: gen2
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: agent-runner@my-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com
      timeoutSeconds: 3600     # up to 1 hour per agent run
      containers:
        - image: us-docker.pkg.dev/my-project/agents/research-agent:abc1234
          resources:
            limits:
              cpu: "2"
              memory: 2Gi
          env:
            - name: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: anthropic-api-key   # GCP Secret Manager secret
                  key: latest

a legal-tech company runs a contract review agent that processes uploaded PDFs. Users upload a file through a web app, which enqueues a review task on SQS. An ECS Fargate cluster of agent containers picks up tasks, sends document chunks to Claude via the Anthropic API, extracts clauses, and stores a structured JSON report in DynamoDB keyed by task ID. The web app polls the status endpoint until the report is ready, then displays it. On weekday mornings when many contracts are uploaded simultaneously, the ECS service scales from 2 to 40 containers within three minutes based on SQS queue depth.

"""Contract review agent — end-to-end task handler."""

import anthropic
import boto3
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict

@dataclass
class ClauseExtraction:
    clause_type: str
    text: str
    risk_level: str  # "low" | "medium" | "high"
    page: int

def run_contract_review(client: anthropic.Anthropic, document_text: str) -> list[ClauseExtraction]:
    """Send contract text to Claude and extract structured clause data."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=4096,
        system=(
            "You are a contract review assistant. Extract all notable clauses from the contract. "
            "Return a JSON array of objects with fields: clause_type, text, risk_level (low/medium/high), page."
        ),
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": f"Review this contract and extract all notable clauses:\n\n{document_text}"
        }],
    )
    raw = json.loads(response.content[0].text)
    return [ClauseExtraction(**c) for c in raw]

def handle_task(task_body: dict) -> None:
    """Called once per SQS message. Stores results in DynamoDB."""
    from src.secrets import load_secrets
    secrets = load_secrets()
    anthropic_client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=secrets["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"])

    # Fetch PDF text from S3
    s3 = boto3.client("s3")
    obj = s3.get_object(Bucket=task_body["bucket"], Key=task_body["key"])
    document_text = obj["Body"].read().decode("utf-8")  # assume pre-extracted text

    clauses = run_contract_review(anthropic_client, document_text)

    # Persist result to DynamoDB
    ddb = boto3.resource("dynamodb")
    table = ddb.Table("AgentResults")
    table.put_item(Item={
        "task_id": task_body["task_id"],
        "status": "completed",
        "clauses": [asdict(c) for c in clauses],
        "clause_count": len(clauses),
    })
Visibility timeout shorter than task duration:

if a task takes 4 minutes but the visibility timeout is 90 seconds, SQS re-delivers the message to another container while the first is still running. The result is duplicate executions and wasted LLM spend. Set visibility timeout to at least 2x the maximum expected task duration.
Putting secrets in environment variables at deploy time:

environment variables set in ECS task definitions or Kubernetes manifests appear in plain text in the console and in deployment logs. Always use the secrets manager reference syntax so the value is fetched at runtime and never exposed as a string in config files.

without a DLQ, a malformed message that always causes the agent to crash loops indefinitely, consuming retries and burning cost. Configure SQS to move messages to a DLQ after 3–5 failed attempts and alert on DLQ depth.
and a bug triggers 10,000 tasks simultaneously, your LLM API quota is exhausted and your bill spikes before the problem is detected. Set a
consistent with your API rate limits and set a billing alert.

writing intermediate agent results to the container's local filesystem means they are lost if the container is replaced by the scheduler. All mutable state must go to S3, DynamoDB, or another managed store outside the container.

role (common shortcut when debugging) means a prompt-injection attack or dependency vulnerability can be escalated to full cloud account access. Scope each role to exactly the services and resources the agent uses.
One agent type per container image:

separating research agents, document-processing agents, and notification agents into distinct images means you can scale and deploy each independently without risking a regression in an unrelated agent.

SQS provides at-least-once delivery. Design task handlers so that processing the same message twice produces the same result — use the task ID as an idempotency key when writing to the state store (conditional writes in DynamoDB,
Emit structured log events per agent step:

log a JSON object at the start of each LLM call (model, input tokens) and at the end (output tokens, latency, tool results). This data drives cost attribution, latency analysis, and debugging without requiring a code change to extract.

compile dependencies in a builder stage, copy only the final artifacts to a slim runtime image. This reduces image size (faster cold starts, smaller attack surface) and keeps build secrets out of the final image layer.
Pin image tags to SHAs in production:
in production task definitions. Mutable tags mean a new push can silently change what the production service runs.

interactive agent requests (user is waiting) should go to a dedicated high-priority queue with more consumers, while batch background tasks go to a separate low-priority queue. A single queue means a batch job backlog can delay real-time users.

The dominant latency factor for cloud-deployed agents is the LLM API round-trip, not infrastructure overhead. A Claude API call for a 2,000-token input and 1,000-token output takes 3–8 seconds. Container startup, SQS polling, and DynamoDB writes add roughly 100–300 ms combined — within the noise. The focus should be on
(prompt compression, prompt caching, streaming) rather than infrastructure micro-optimization.

Throughput scales linearly with the number of containers up to the LLM API rate limit. If the rate limit is 1,000 requests per minute and each agent task makes 5 LLM calls, the maximum throughput is 200 completed tasks per minute regardless of container count. Plan your autoscaling ceiling accordingly and request API tier upgrades before deploying high-concurrency workloads.

Anthropic's prompt caching feature re-uses the KV cache for repeated system prompt prefixes across API calls. For agents with large fixed system prompts (tool descriptions, instructions), caching reduces latency by 80% and token cost by 90% on cached tokens.
client once at module level (not inside the task handler) so HTTP connections to the Anthropic API are reused across tasks in the same container process.
if the agent embeds document chunks before tool calls, batch multiple chunks per embedding API request rather than one per request. Most embedding APIs support batch sizes of 100–2048 items.

horizontal scaling (more containers) improves throughput for I/O-bound LLM agents. Vertical scaling (larger instance type with more RAM) improves throughput only for CPU/memory-bound tasks like large embedding model inference. Most cloud LLM agents benefit more from horizontal scaling.
Managed agent platform (Bedrock Agents, Vertex AI Agents)
Cloud platform deployment improves both security and reliability compared to a self-managed server, but only if you use the platform's capabilities correctly.

run agent containers in a private subnet with no public IP. All inbound requests enter through the API Gateway / load balancer. The agents call the LLM API and tool endpoints outbound through a NAT gateway. This prevents direct internet access to agent containers.

each ECS task or Cloud Run service account should be limited to the specific S3 buckets, DynamoDB tables, SQS queues, and secret ARNs it uses — not the entire service namespace. Use resource-based conditions (

user-supplied content that reaches the agent's system or user prompt is a prompt injection vector. Validate and sanitize inputs before including them in LLM messages. Consider a separate "guardrails" LLM call that checks the input before the main agent call.

spread ECS tasks or Kubernetes pods across at least two Availability Zones. Cloud providers charge no additional fee for multi-AZ task placement, and it eliminates the most common cause of service outage (AZ-level hardware failure).
in the worker process. When ECS or Kubernetes sends

before stopping a container, stop polling for new messages but finish the current task within the grace period (30 seconds by default). This prevents in-flight tasks from failing due to container replacement.
Circuit breaking for LLM API calls:

if the LLM API returns 5xx errors for N consecutive calls, open a circuit breaker that returns fast failures for the next M seconds rather than queuing up retries. This prevents agent containers from blocking all threads on a stuck upstream and allows the queue depth to stay manageable.

The core pattern is stateless containers + external state: agent logic runs in disposable containers; all durable data (task results, conversation state, artifacts) lives in managed cloud stores outside the container.

Message queues (SQS, Pub/Sub) decouple request ingestion from agent execution. This enables burst handling, at-least-once delivery, dead-lettering, and queue-depth-based autoscaling — none of which a synchronous HTTP server provides.
Set the SQS visibility timeout to at least 2x the maximum expected task duration, configure a dead-letter queue after 3–5 retries, and set a
ceiling consistent with your LLM API rate limit.
Always load API keys from the secrets manager at container startup via IAM role — never embed them in images, environment variables set at deploy time, or source code.
The bottleneck for cloud-deployed LLM agents is almost always LLM API throughput, not infrastructure. Optimize prompt caching, connection pooling, and batching before adding containers.
Design task handlers to be idempotent (same message processed twice → same outcome) because SQS provides at-least-once, not exactly-once, delivery.

For agents that run longer than 15 minutes or require guaranteed resume after failure, layer a durable execution engine (Temporal, Prefect) on top of the cloud platform rather than relying on queue visibility timeout alone.
